d.u: Emperor Ping of Han, Liu Kan

v.t: Wang Mang Appointed as Duke Anhan
v.s: In the first month of the Yuanshi era of the Western Han, Grand Empress Dowager Wang Zhengjun issued an edict granting Wang Mang the title 'Duke Anhan' (Tranquilizing Han Duke). Prior to this, Wang Mang instructed officials in Yizhou to offer a white pheasant in the name of the Yuechang people, using the allusion of the Duke of Zhou assisting King Cheng to create momentum. Court officials submitted memorials praising Wang Mang's virtue as comparable to the Duke of Zhou. After feigning modesty, Wang Mang accepted the title with a fief of 28,000 households. This marked Wang Mang's transcendence of ordinary consort kin status, making him the de facto highest power holder of the Western Han, and a decisive step towards usurping the throne.

v.t: Wang Mang Established the Office of Xihe
v.s: Following the 'Rites of Zhou', Wang Mang restored the ancient official title 'Xihe', granting it significant economic powers such as overseeing the Five Equalisations and Six Controls. Xihe was originally a celestial official under Emperor Yao. Through this, Wang Mang sought historical legitimacy for his 'reforms by entrusting antiquity'. The establishment of Xihe laid the institutional groundwork for Wang Mang's subsequent comprehensive implementation of economic control measures.

v.t: Wang Mang Established the 'Four Assistants' to Control the Court
v.s: Imitating the 'Rites of Zhou', Wang Mang established the positions of Grand Tutor, Grand Mentor, Grand Protector, and Junior Tutor as the 'Four Assistants', all held by trusted confidants and renowned scholars. Ostensibly assisting the young Emperor Ping of Han, these four individuals were actually manipulated by Wang Mang to control the court, further centralizing power.

v.t: Posthumous Honoring of Confucius and His Descendants
v.s: To win over the scholar-officials, Grand Empress Dowager Wang Zhengjun and Wang Mang posthumously honored Confucius as 'Baocheng Xuan Ni Gong' (Proclaiming Accomplishment and Illustrious Ni Duke) and enfeoffed Confucius's descendant Kong Jun as 'Baocheng Hou' (Proclaiming Accomplishment Marquis) with a fief of 2,000 households. This was the first official enfeoffment of Confucius's descendants in Chinese history, a practice continued by subsequent dynasties, thereby elevating the official status of Confucianism.

v.t: Liu Xin Compiled the 'Santong Calendar'
v.s: The Western Han scholar Liu Xin, continuing the academic tradition of his father Liu Xiang, compiled the 'Santong Calendar' (Triple Concordance Calendar System). This was China's first complete theoretical work on calendrical science, detailing the movements of the sun and moon, and methods for calculating solar terms and intercalary months. Considered a precursor to the world's earliest astronomical almanac, the 'Santong Calendar' had a profound influence on later calendars.

v.t: Tiberius Suppressed the Germanic Rebellion
v.s: The Roman general Tiberius (the future Roman emperor) led forces to suppress a rebellion by Germanic tribes. Using a combination of stratagems and military deterrence, he consolidated the Rhine frontier, laying the foundation for the stabilization of the province of Germania. Tiberius's military success also accumulated significant political capital and military achievements for his eventual succession to Augustus's throne.

v.t: Gaius Caesar Assumed the Consulship and Led an Eastern Expedition
v.s: Grandson of Roman Emperor Augustus, Gaius Caesar assumed the consulship and was subsequently sent to the East to handle border affairs concerning Armenia and Parthia. Leading his army to the Euphrates River, he demonstrated Roman deterrence through diplomatic negotiations. This was a crucial step in Augustus's succession planning.

v.t: Establishment of the Power Base for the Precursor of the Kushan Empire
v.s: The Guishuang (Kushan) tribe, one of the five Xihou of the Yuezhi, under the leadership of Qiujiuque (Kujula Kadphises), gradually unified the various tribes, forming a powerful regime in the Bactria region of Central Asia. This laid a solid foundation for the future establishment of the Kushan Empire.

v.t: Birth of Jesus (Traditional Dating)
v.s: According to the Anno Domini era system, the year AD 1 was designated as the year of the birth of Jesus Christ. This calendar era was proposed by the 6th-century monk Dionysius Exiguus and later promoted by Pope Gregory XIII, becoming the basis for the Gregorian calendar. Modern scholars believe Jesus was likely born between 6 and 4 BC, but AD 1 as the starting point of the era remains widely adopted.

d.u: Emperor Ping of Han, Liu Kan

v.t: Wang Mang Donated Estates to Relieve Drought and Locust Disasters
v.s: A severe drought and locust plague struck the Guandong region, causing widespread displacement. Grand Marshal Wang Mang took the lead by donating his estates in Chang'an to shelter victims and funding the establishment of temporary housing and medical quarantine facilities in the city, one of the world's earliest quarantine measures. This move won him immense prestige, though it was essentially a political performance to pave the way for usurping power.

v.t: County Locust Capture Rewarded with Cash
v.s: The Western Han court implemented a policy rewarding people with cash based on the amount of locusts they captured, allowing commoners to exchange captured locusts for money. This was one of the world's earliest incentive measures for locust control, demonstrating innovation in agricultural pest management.

v.t: Converted Imperial Parks into Counties to Settle Refugees
v.s: To cope with severe natural disasters and refugee problems, the Western Han court converted some imperial hunting parks into regular counties, distributing the land to displaced peasants for cultivation, stabilizing society by releasing state-owned land.

v.t: Extensive Enfeoffment of Liu Imperial Clan Members as Kings
v.s: To win over the Liu clan and reduce resistance, Wang Mang used the court's authority to extensively enfeoff Liu imperial clan members as kings and marquises. Ostensibly honoring the Han house, this was actually a political strategy to divide and weaken the Liu family.

v.t: Wang Mang Plotted to Make His Daughter Empress
v.s: To further consolidate his power, Wang Mang planned to marry his daughter to Emperor Ping of Han. He first had his confidants propose establishing an empress, then feigned modesty. Eventually, under public 'petition', the Grand Empress Dowager issued an edict making the Wang daughter empress, greatly enhancing his status as a consort kin.

v.t: Huangzhi State Presented a Rhino
v.s: Envoys from the distant southern state of Huangzhi traveled thousands of miles by sea to Chang'an, presenting rare beasts like rhinos. The Han court saw this as a symbol of its far-reaching prestige, and Wang莽used it to proclaim his achievements in governing the state as comparable to ancient sage kings.

v.t: Gaius Caesar Met with Parthia on the Euphrates
v.s: Gaius Caesar, Rome's plenipotentiary for the East, met with Parthian King Phraates V on an island in the Euphrates River. They recognized each other's spheres of influence, temporarily resolving the issue of Armenian succession, and maintaining peace for about twenty years.

v.t: Parthian Queen Musa Co-ruled with Her Son
v.s: A unique political arrangement emerged in the Parthian (Arsacid) Empire: Queen Musa co-ruled with her son, Phraates V. Musa, originally a Roman slave girl, became queen through her beauty and intelligence, effectively wielding power, highlighting the complexities of Parthian court politics.

v.t: Lucius Caesar Died in Massalia
v.s: Grandson of Roman Emperor Augustus, Lucius Caesar, died of illness in Massalia (modern-day Marseille) at the age of 19 while en route to Spain. The premature death of him and his brother Gaius forced Augustus to reconsider his succession plans.

d.u: Emperor Ping of Han, Liu Kan

v.t: Wang Mang's Daughter Established as Empress
v.s: After repeated petitions from court officials, Wang Mang's daughter was officially established as Empress of Emperor Ping of Han. Grand Empress Dowager Wang Zhengjun approved under pressure. Through this political marriage, Wang Mang tightly bound himself to the Han imperial house, making his consort kin position unassailable.

v.t: Established Regulations for Carriages, Attire, and Marriage
v.s: Using the 'Rites of Zhou' as a blueprint, Wang Mang established strict hierarchical regulations for carriages and clothing, specifying styles and colors according to official rank. He also standardized marriage rituals and unified standards for betrothal gifts and dowries. Aimed at restoring social order through ancient rites, this laid the groundwork for his 'reforms by entrusting antiquity'.

v.t: Extensive Enfeoffment of Imperial Clan and Meritorious Descendants
v.s: To further consolidate his position, Wang Mang used the court's authority to extensively enfeoff descendants of the Han imperial clan and meritorious officials, granting noble titles and lands, winning over people's hearts and garnering support, thereby accumulating capital for his eventual dictatorship and usurpation.

v.t: Flourishing Silk Road Trade Between East and West
v.s: Chinese silk, lacquerware, and ironware were transported by Sogdian caravans to Persia and Rome, while western glassware, gold and silver artifacts, jewelry, and spices were imported into China. The Silk Road was not only a trade route but also a major artery for the exchange of technology, religion, and art, promoting mutual understanding among Eurasian civilizations.

v.t: Phraates V Deposed by Parthian Nobles
v.s: King Phraates V of Parthia was deposed by a coalition of conservative nobles due to his Hellenizing policies and co-rule with his mother Musa. This reflected the intensifying conflict between Hellenized and conservative factions within the empire.

v.t: Forum of Augustus Dedicated in Rome
v.s: Roman Emperor Augustus built the Forum of Augustus between the Palatine and Capitoline Hills, featuring marble colonnades and a gilded bronze statue of himself in a quadriga at its center. The forum housed a library with Greek and Latin sections and a senate meeting hall, symbolizing the prosperity and political legitimacy of the Pax Romana.

d.u: Emperor Ping of Han, Liu Kan

v.t: Wang Mang Given the Title 'Zaiheng'
v.s: The Grand Empress Dowager issued an edict granting Wang Mang the title 'Zaiheng', ranking him above all feudal kings. This title was created by Wang Mang himself, taking the meaning of 'Yi Yin as Eheng, the Duke of Zhou as Da Zai', implying his achievements were comparable to those of the sage assistants of antiquity.

v.t: Expanded the Imperial Academy and Mingtang Biyong
v.s: To advertise his reverence for the rites of the Duke of Zhou, Wang Mang massively expanded the Imperial Academy in Chang'an, constructing new buildings such as the Mingtang (for proclamations), Biyong (for ritual music and education), and Lingtai (for astronomical observation), demonstrating his determination to restore the Rites of Zhou.

v.t: Sent Envoys to Observe Customs and Collect Poetry
v.s: Wang Mang sent envoys to various regions to understand local conditions and collect folk songs. Nominally following the Zhou dynasty practice of 'collecting poetry to observe popular sentiment', it was actually a political performance to publicize his effective governance and the submission of all under heaven.

v.t: Augustus Enacted Social Reforms
v.s: Roman Emperor Augustus issued a series of laws imposing additional taxes on the unmarried and childless, while providing rewards for families with multiple children, aiming to restore traditional marriage and family values, and rectify social morals.

v.t: Parthian King Orodes III Deposed
v.s: In AD 4, King Orodes III of the Parthian (Arsacid) Empire was deposed by the nobility. Parthia was in turmoil with intense factional struggles among the aristocracy. The deposition of Orodes III led to a brief power vacuum. The Roman Empire took the opportunity to expand its influence in Armenia. The succession crisis in Parthia continued until AD 6 when Vologases I finally stabilized the situation.

d.u: Emperor Ping of Han, Liu Kan

v.t: Emperor Ping of Han Suddenly Died
v.s: The 14-year-old Emperor Ping of Han, Liu Kan, suddenly died in the Weiyang Palace. According to the 'Book of Han', when Emperor Ping fell ill, Wang Mang 'composed a prayer offering himself in the emperor's place at the Tai altar', but the emperor still died. Later historians widely suspect Wang Mang poisoned Emperor Ping to install the two-year-old Ruzi Ying as crown prince, acting as 'acting emperor' and clearing the way for his usurpation.

v.t: Wang Mang Became Regent, Installed Ruzi Ying
v.s: After Emperor Ping's death, Wang Mang bypassed older imperial clan members, choosing the two-year-old Liu Ying as Crown Prince, assuming the title 'Jia Huangdi' (Acting Emperor) as regent, and changing the era name to Jushe. This marked that Wang Mang was only one step away from formal usurpation.

v.t: Ovid Completed 'Metamorphoses'
v.s: The Roman poet Ovid completed his narrative poem 'Metamorphoses', telling over 250 Greco-Roman myths from the creation of the world to the deification of Julius Caesar, centering on the theme of 'transformation'. With its exquisite narrative and beautiful language, it became a classic of Latin literature, profoundly influencing later European literature and art.

v.t: Tiberius's Germanic War Concluded
v.s: In AD 5, Roman general Tiberius's military operations in Germania achieved interim results. He led Roman legions deep into the Elbe River valley, conquering several Germanic tribes. Tiberius employed a strategy combining diplomacy and military force, establishing an administrative system for the province. However, after the Battle of Teutoburg Forest in AD 9, Rome was forced to abandon its plans to conquer all of Germania, withdrawing its border to the Rhine River line.

v.t: Temple of Olympian Zeus Destroyed by Fire
v.s: The Temple of Olympian Zeus in Olympia, Greece (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), was severely damaged by a great fire. Built between 470-456 BC, it housed the colossal chryselephantine statue of Zeus by Phidias. The fire destroyed the building, but the statue was rescued and moved to Constantinople, where it was later destroyed in a fire in AD 475.

v.t: Wang Mang Declared 'Acting Emperor' and Changed Era to Jushe
v.s: In AD 6, after poisoning Emperor Ping, Wang Mang installed the two-year-old Ruzi Ying as crown prince, declared himself 'Jia Huangdi' (Acting Emperor), and changed the era name to Jushe. Court officials addressed him as 'She Huangdi' (Regent Emperor). He used imperial ritual in sacrifices and court assemblies, with power equal to that of a true emperor. This was one of the most crucial steps in Wang Mang's path to usurpation.

v.t: Liu Chong's Revolt Against Wang Mang Failed
v.s: Liu Chong, a member of the Han imperial clan, raised troops in Nanyang to attack Wang Mang. Although he gained some support from other Liu clan members and officials, his forces were insufficient and he was quickly suppressed. This was the first open armed action against Wang Mang.

v.t: Wang Mang Enfeoffed Imperial Clan to Appease the Liu Family
v.s: In AD 6, while acting as acting emperor, Wang Mang enfeoffed numerous Liu imperial clan members and descendants of meritorious officials to win support and appease those dissatisfied with his usurpation. He enfeoffed dozens of Liu clan members as marquises and restored sacrifices for the Han imperial house, attempting to reduce resentment from the Liu clan.

v.t: Judea Became a Directly Ruled Roman Province
v.s: After deposing the Jewish king Herod Archelaus, Roman Emperor Augustus formally incorporated Judea as a directly ruled Roman province (Province of Judea), sending a governor to rule directly. The first governor, Coponius, conducted a census to levy a poll tax, sparking armed resistance led by Judas of Galilee, increasing tensions that eventually led to the Jewish War of 66-70 AD.

v.t: Augustus Established the Military Treasury
v.s: Roman Emperor Augustus established the 'aerarium militare' (military treasury) to specifically pay pensions and land compensation to retired soldiers. Its funding sources included a 5% inheritance tax and a 1% auction sales tax, ensuring the army's loyalty to the emperor.

v.t: Zhai Yi Revolted, Made Liu Xin Emperor
v.s: The Prefect of Dong Commandery, Zhai Yi, raised troops against Wang Mang in Henan, installing a descendant of the Han imperial house, Liu Xin, as the Son of Heaven. He issued a proclamation calling on the realm to attack Wang Mang, and his forces quickly swelled to over one hundred thousand men.

v.t: Wang Yi et al. Decisively Defeated Zhai Yi's Army
v.s: In AD 7, Wang Mang sent Wang Yi, Sun Jian, and others to suppress the revolt of Zhai Yi and Liu Xin. Zhai Yi had assembled over one hundred thousand men in Chenliu, creating a powerful force. Wang Yi employed a tactic of converging columns and launched a surprise attack, defeating Zhai Yi's army at Yuxian (modern-day Qixian, Henan). Zhai Yi was captured and executed. This victory eliminated the greatest military threat to Wang Mang's path to usurpation.

v.t: Panicked Wang Mang Wrote a Prayer to Heaven
v.s: Faced with the anti-Wang Mang wave led by Zhai Yi, Zhao Ming, Huo Hong, and others, Wang Mang became distraught. He wrote a prayer and proclamation to Heaven professing his loyalty, reflecting his growing reliance on superstitious means to stabilize people's hearts.

v.t: Tiberius Ended Germanic Campaigns and Returned to Rome
v.s: In AD 7, Tiberius ended his military campaigns in Germania and returned to Rome. He had opened new provinces for Rome, captured tens of thousands of Germans, and established military bases on the right bank of the Rhine. Upon his return, Tiberius celebrated a triumph and was designated by Augustus as one of his successors.

v.t: Construction of Roman Germanic Frontier Defenses
v.s: The Roman Empire began constructing border fortifications (Limes) between the Rhine and Danube rivers, consisting of forts, watchtowers, and walls, forming a crucial part of the empire's frontier defense system.

v.t: Wang Mang Replaced Han and Established the Xin Dynasty
v.s: Wang Mang forced Grand Empress Dowager Wang Zhengjun to surrender the Imperial Seal, ascended the throne as Son of Heaven at the front hall of Weiyang Palace, and changed the national title to 'Xin' (New). The Western Han dynasty ended after 214 years. This was the first dynastic transition completed through peaceful abdication in Chinese history.

v.t: Ai Zhang of Zitong Presented the Golden Casket Diagram
v.s: Ai Zhang of Zitong manufactured and presented a 'heaven-sent talisman document' - the Golden Casket Diagram (Jinkui Tuce) - predicting that Wang Mang should become emperor. With Wang Mang's instigation or tacit approval, such auspicious omens and prophecy texts constantly appeared, providing public opinion that 'Heaven's mandate' favored Wang Mang's succession.

v.t: Ruzi Ying Deposed as Duke of Ding'an
v.s: In AD 8, after formally declaring himself emperor and establishing the Xin dynasty, Wang Mang deposed Ruzi Liu Ying, granting him the title 'Duke of Ding'an'. Liu Ying was confined to a specific residence, his family forbidden from communicating with him. When Liu Ying grew up, he could not even distinguish the six domestic animals. Wang Mang kept Liu clan members under strict surveillance, and the Western Han imperial house completely lost power.

v.t: Egyptian Grain Shipped from Alexandria to Rome
v.s: Egypt, as the 'breadbasket of Rome', shipped about 20 million modii (approximately 120 million liters) of wheat from Alexandria to Rome annually, feeding hundreds of thousands of commoners receiving free grain rations. This maritime grain lifeline was crucial for the stability of the Roman Empire.

v.t: Augustus Conducted a Roman Census
v.s: Roman Emperor Augustus conducted an empire-wide census, counting the number and property of citizens, recording the results in the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti' (Deeds of the Divine Augustus), providing a basis for taxation and military conscription.

v.t: Vonones Became King of Parthia
v.s: Vonones I ascended the Parthian throne with the support of the Parthian nobility. Having lived as a hostage in Rome for many years, he was deeply influenced by Roman culture. He attempted to implement Hellenizing reforms after his accession, provoking strong dissatisfaction among conservative nobles.

v.t: Wang Mang Reformed Official Titles and Ranks
v.s: After formally declaring himself emperor, Wang Mang undertook a large-scale reform of the official system and noble ranks based on the 'Rites of Zhou': establishing Four Assistants, Three Dukes, Four Generals at the center, changing many local commandery and county names to ancient titles, and dividing noble ranks into five grades: Gong, Hou, Bo, Zi, Nan. These antiquarian reforms were divorced from reality, causing administrative chaos and exacerbating the ruling crisis.

v.t: Wang Mang Issued an Edict Reforming the Land System (Wangtian Decree)
v.s: Wang Mang issued the 'Wangtian Decree', declaring all land nationwide state-owned and renamed 'Wangtian', forbidding buying and selling. Families with fewer than eight members could not own more than one 'jing' (a unit of land area), with excess to be distributed among clan members and neighbors. Aimed at solving land annexation, the decree was strongly resisted by powerful families and was forced to be abolished three years later.

v.t: Wang Mang Implemented Currency Reforms
v.s: Wang Mang carried out currency reforms, minting new coin types such as large coins, inscribed knife coins, and inscribed 'wrong' knife coins. The chaotic currency system and currency devaluation severely impacted the civilian economy, leading to soaring prices and hardship for both farmers and merchants, becoming an important reason for the rapid collapse of the Xin dynasty.

v.t: Banned the Buying and Selling of Slaves
v.s: Wang Mang issued a decree prohibiting the buying and selling of slaves nationwide, reflecting the Confucian principle of benevolence that 'human nature is the most precious'. However, powerful local families strongly resisted, making enforcement difficult, and the ban ultimately proved ineffectual.

v.t: Battle of the Teutoburg Forest
v.s: Roman governor of Germania, Varus, was leading the 17th, 18th, and 19th legions (about 20,000 men) through the Teutoburg Forest when they were ambushed by Germanic leader Arminius. The terrain prevented Roman tactics from working. After three days of intense fighting, the Romans were annihilated, and Varus committed suicide. This disastrous defeat caused Augustus to 'cry out in grief, and for months he left his hair and beard unkempt'. Rome permanently abandoned plans to conquer Germania.

v.t: Wang Mang's Second Currency Reform
v.s: Wang Mang launched a second currency reform, abolishing the inscribed knife and 'wrong' knife coins, and minting small coins and large fifty coins for concurrent use. The overvaluation of the large coins led to widespread counterfeiting, creating even more economic chaos.

v.t: Wang Mang Changed the Title of the Xiongnu Chanyu
v.s: To display the Xin dynasty's authority and belittle the Xiongnu, Wang Mang changed the Xiongnu Chanyu's title to 'Jiangnu Fuyu' (Submissive Slave Servant). This insulting title change infuriated the Xiongnu Chanyu, leading to a sharp deterioration in Han-Xiongnu relations and renewed warfare along the frontier.

v.t: Tiberius Returned to the Germanic Front
v.s: After the Teutoburg disaster, Augustus urgently sent Tiberius back to Germania to stabilize the situation. Tiberius strengthened the Rhine defenses and used divide-and-rule tactics to break up the Germanic tribal alliance, successfully preventing Germanic incursions into Gaul.

v.t: Continued Academic Prosperity in Alexandria, Egypt
v.s: Alexandria, under Roman rule, remained a Mediterranean center of learning. Although the Library of Alexandria had been damaged, it was still the largest collection of books. The Mouseion (temple of the Muses) gathered scholars from around the world, advancing knowledge in astronomy, mathematics, medicine, geography, and philosophy.

v.t: Completion of the Roman Road System
v.s: The Roman Empire continuously improved its road network, with total length exceeding 80,000 kilometers by the early 1st century AD. Each road had standard specifications, with milestones marking distances and waystations providing horse changes and lodging, greatly facilitating trade and military mobilization.

v.t: Wang Mang Ordered a Major Conscription
v.s: To address border tensions and domestic rebellions, Wang Mang ordered a nationwide conscription. The heavy burden of military service and corvée labor further aggravated social conflicts, causing many peasants to flee their land and join the anti-Wang Mang rebel armies.

v.t: Abolished Military Agriculture in Wuyuan and Other Commanderies
v.s: Wang Mang ordered the abolition of military agricultural colonies in Wuyuan Commandery and other northern frontier regions. These colonies had been important for supplying food to border defense forces, and their abolition weakened the ability of the border areas to feed themselves.

v.t: Arminius United the Germanic Tribes
v.s: In AD 11, Arminius, chief of the Germanic Cherusci tribe, after his great victory over the Roman legions at Teutoburg Forest, continued to unify neighboring Germanic tribes. He formed an anti-Roman alliance including the Bructeri and Marsi. This move fundamentally checked the Roman Empire's expansion into Germania.

v.t: Rome Established the Province of Pannonia
v.s: Rome established the province of Pannonia in the middle Danube region. Strategically located in Central Europe, it was a forward defensive position against Germanic tribes. Rome built numerous military camps and colonial cities there.

v.t: Wang Mang Implemented Administrative Reforms
v.s: Following the 'Rites of Zhou', Wang Mang reformed the administrative divisions, changing the country into nine provinces and 125 commanderies, and renaming many places with ancient names. He also changed the titles of commandery governors to 'Dayin' and county magistrates to 'Zai'. These formalistic reforms caused administrative chaos, as many officials and commoners were unfamiliar with the new names, leading to numerous errors in official correspondence.

v.t: Proposal to Move the Capital to Luoyang
v.s: Wang Mang once considered moving the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang to align with the concept of 'center of the world' in the 'Rites of Zhou'. He sent officials to survey terrain in Luoyang and prepare for palace construction. However, the enormous cost and opposition from various quarters ultimately shelved the plan. This proposal reflected Wang Mang's tendency towards blind antiquarianism.

v.t: Western Regions Kingdoms Rebelled Against Xin and Submitted to Xiongnu
v.s: After Wang Mang declared himself emperor, he changed his policy towards the various kingdoms of the Western Regions, demoting their kingly titles to 'Marquis'. This aroused strong discontent. Yānqí (Karashahr) was the first to rebel, killing the Xin dynasty's Protector General of the Western Regions, Dan Qin. Subsequently, various kingdoms submitted to the Xiongnu. Wang Mang sent an expedition to punish them but was defeated, and the Silk Road was interrupted. This was a major failure of Wang Mang's foreign policy.

v.t: Parthian Civil War: Rise of Artabanus II
v.s: Power struggles were intense within the Parthian Empire. Artabanus II, the governor of Media, defeated his rivals and rose to prominence, later becoming king of Parthia. He successfully challenged Roman hegemony in the East.

v.t: Wangtian Land System Forced to Abolish
v.s: The 'Wangtian Land System' (well-field system) promoted by Wang Mang met with strong resistance from powerful landlords. After land was confiscated, peasants did not receive redistributed land, but instead lost the freedom to buy and sell land due to the prohibition on transactions. Combined with official corruption and malpractice, the social economy fell into chaos. Wang Mang was forced to order a suspension of the Wangtian Land System in AD 13.

v.t: Yānqí Rebelled Against Xin and Killed the Protector General
v.s: In the fifth year of Wang Mang's Shijianguo era (AD 13), Yānqí (modern Yānqí, Xinjiang), dissatisfied with Wang Mang's demotion of its kingly title to 'Marquis', rebelled and killed the Xin dynasty's Protector General of the Western Regions, Dan Qin. Wang Mang sent General of the Five Mights, Wang Jun, to lead an expedition, but they were defeated by Yānqí in alliance with the Xiongnu. Thereafter, the various kingdoms of the Western Regions submitted to the Xiongnu one after another, and Wang Mang completely lost control of the region.

v.t: Artabanus II Defeated Vonones
v.s: Artabanus II defeated Vonones I in the Parthian civil war, becoming the new king of the Arsacid Empire. He was a strong monarch who worked to restore Parthian influence in the East.

v.t: Wang Mang Changed Era to Tianfeng
v.s: In AD 14, Wang Mang changed the era name to 'Tianfeng', using prophetic signs to consolidate his rule. At the same time, Wang Mang implemented the fourth currency reform, issuing two new currencies: 'Huobu' and 'Huquan'. The Huobu weighed 25 zhu and was worth 25 Huquan coins. However, the new currency system remained chaotic, and barter continued to dominate civilian transactions, with the economic situation worsening daily.

v.t: Wang Mang Re-established Xihe Officials and Implemented the Five Equalisations and Six Controls
v.s: Wang Mang implemented the 'Five Equalisations and Six Controls' system: the government controlled the management of salt, iron, liquor, coinage, credit, and famous mountains and marshes. This was the core of the state monopoly economic policy, further intensifying social contradictions.

v.t: Augustus Died, Tiberius Succeeded
v.s: The founding Roman Emperor Augustus died of illness in Nola at the age of 75. He reigned for 41 years, ending long-standing civil wars, establishing the Principate, and ushering in the 'Pax Romana' era. The Senate deified him, and Tiberius succeeded him as emperor.

v.t: Mutiny of the Germanic Legions
v.s: News of Augustus's death reached the Germanic front, and the legions stationed along the Rhine mutinied due to harsh conditions. Germanicus skillfully suppressed the mutiny and led a counterattack against Germanic tribes, consolidating the Rhine frontier.

v.t: Wang Mang Changed Official Titles Again
v.s: Wang Mang once again massively changed the names of central and local official positions, changing many to ancient titles from the 'Rites of Zhou', such as changing 'Da Sinong' (Minister of Finance) to 'Xihe' and 'Taishou' (Commandery Governor) to 'Dayin', causing administrative confusion.

v.t: Peasant Uprising in Wuyuan and Dai Commanderies
v.s: A large-scale peasant uprising broke out in Wuyuan and Dai commanderies under Xin rule. Due to oppressive policies, heavy corvée labor, and consecutive years of drought, the people of the northern border areas could no longer bear the burden and rose in revolt.

v.t: Kujula Kadphises United the Five Yuezhi Tribes
v.s: Around AD 15, Kujula Kadphises, chief of the Kushan tribe (one of the five Yuezhi Xihou), unified the other four tribes through war and diplomacy. Kujula Kadphises established his capital at Bactra (modern Balkh, Afghanistan), proclaimed himself 'King of Kings', and founded the Kushan Empire. He issued gold coins in the Roman style and controlled Silk Road trade.

v.t: Romanization of Southern Britain
v.s: The Roman Empire promoted Romanization in southern Britain. Law, currency, language, and construction techniques were gradually introduced, and local Celtic aristocrats began to adopt the Roman way of life.

v.t: Wang Mang's Campaign Against the Southwestern Yi Failed
v.s: Wang Mang dispatched General Lian Dan to lead an expedition against the Jutian Kingdom of the Southwestern Yi. Due to the difficult terrain, long supply lines, and stubborn resistance of the tribal peoples, the Xin forces suffered heavy losses, deepening the fiscal crisis.

v.t: Wang Mang Established the 'Classic of Music' as an Official Subject
v.s: To perfect the 'Six Classics' system (the Classics of Poetry, History, Changes, Rites, and Spring and Autumn Annals lacked the Classic of Music), Wang Mang had the musical literature compiled by scholars since the late Western Han established as the 'Classic of Music', setting up an Erudite (Boshi) position to teach it. He believed that restoring the 'Classic of Music' would perfect ritual and music to civilize the world. However, the 'Classic of Music' did not truly survive in later generations.

v.t: Germanicus Won Another Great Victory
v.s: The Roman general Germanicus defeated the Germanic allied forces led by Arminius at the Battle of Idistaviso in Germania, achieving a significant victory.

v.t: Roman-Dacian War
v.s: The Roman Empire and the Kingdom of Dacia clashed in the lower Danube region. Roman forces attempted to punish Dacian raiding, but the fighting was indecisive, setting the stage for Trajan's later full-scale conquest of Dacia.

v.t: Outbreak of the Lülin (Greenwood) Rebellion
v.s: Due to consecutive years of famine in the Jingzhou region, starving peasants led by Wang Kuang and Wang Feng gathered to rebel, using Mount Lülin as their base. They were called the 'Lülin Army' and quickly grew to tens of thousands, becoming one of the main anti-Wang Mang forces.

v.t: Guo Tianyi of Linhuai Rebelled
v.s: Guo Tianyi from Linhuai Commandery gathered followers and rebelled in the Kuaiji region. This was one of the earliest peasant uprisings during the Xin dynasty, using mountainous terrain as a base and repeatedly defeating government forces, opening the prelude to nationwide anti-Wang Mang revolts.

v.t: Wang Mang Sent People to Seek Immortals and Magical Arts
v.s: Imitating Emperor Wu of Han, Wang Mang sent numerous magicians across the country to search for immortals and the arts of longevity. He held sacrificial ceremonies for immortals in the palace, attempting to maintain his ruling confidence through mysticism.

v.t: Germanicus Recalled to Rome
v.s: In AD 17, Roman Emperor Tiberius, fearing that Germanicus's successes in Germania might threaten his position, recalled him to Rome. After achieving major military victories (including capturing Arminius's stronghold), Germanicus was ordered to go east to handle affairs in Armenia. He died mysteriously in Antioch in AD 19.

v.t: Outbreak of the Red Eyebrow Rebellion
v.s: Fan Chong from Langye Commandery gathered followers and rebelled in Ju County. To distinguish themselves from government troops, they dyed their eyebrows red and were therefore called the 'Red Eyebrow Army', rapidly developing into a huge armed force numbering hundreds of thousands.

v.t: Diao Zidu of Donghai Rebelled
v.s: Diao Zidu from Donghai Commandery gathered followers and rebelled, leading starving peasants to attack government offices and open granaries for relief. This was an important component of the anti-Wang Mang uprising in the east, echoing the Lülin and Red Eyebrow forces.

v.t: Lülin Army Decisively Defeated Jingzhou Government Forces
v.s: In AD 18, due to consecutive years of famine, the Lülin Rebellion led by Wang Kuang and Wang Feng erupted in Jingzhou. The rebel forces defeated the encirclement campaign by Jingzhou government forces at Mount Lülin (modern Dangyang, Hubei), rapidly expanding to tens of thousands. This victory marked the beginning of the overthrow of the Xin regime. Subsequently, the Lülin Army split into several groups, including the Xinshi and Pinglin forces.

v.t: Arminius Assassinated
v.s: The Germanic hero Arminius was assassinated by his own family members due to internal power struggles. His death led to the disintegration of the Germanic tribal alliance, reducing pressure on the Roman frontier.

v.t: Great Famine in Guandong
v.s: The Guandong region (eastern Henan, western Shandong) under Xin rule suffered a severe drought and locust plague, leading to total crop failure, widespread starvation, and even reports of cannibalism. Local officials concealed the disaster, and the Wang Mang government was unable to provide relief, causing large numbers of famine victims to join the rebel armies, accelerating the collapse of the Xin dynasty.

v.t: Wang Mang Cast the 'Wei Dou' as a Symbol of Power
v.s: Wang Mang ordered the casting of a 'Wei Dou' (Power Dipper) from bronze, imitating the shape of the Northern Dipper constellation, believing it was the 'celestial pivot' and that the Dipper symbolized his authority coming from Heaven's mandate. Each time he traveled, he had the Wei Dou carried before him to display his sanctity.

v.t: Germanicus Sent East
v.s: Emperor Tiberius sent Germanicus to the East to handle affairs in Armenia and Syria. Ostensibly an important assignment, it was actually a move to remove him from the Germanic legions and diminish his power.

v.t: Intensifying Social Contradictions in the Province of Judea
v.s: Social contradictions were sharp in the Roman province of Judea: repressive Roman policies, heavy taxation, and provocations against Jewish customs aroused strong discontent, while internal Jewish factions (Sadducees, Pharisees, Zealots) fought among themselves, sowing the seeds for the future Jewish War.

v.t: Wang Mang Changed Era to Dihuang, Began Major Palace Construction
v.s: In AD 20, Wang Mang changed the era name to Dihuang, taking the name of one of the Three Sovereigns of high antiquity to mythologize his rule. Simultaneously, he began large-scale construction of palaces and ancestral temples in Chang'an and Luoyang, conscripting a huge number of laborers and exhausting vast amounts of state treasury funds, further increasing the burden on the people and social conflicts.

v.t: Germanicus Died in Syria
v.s: The Roman commander for the East, Germanicus, died suddenly in Antioch, Syria, at the age of 33, suspected of being poisoned by the governor Piso, acting on Tiberius's orders. His death shocked Rome, and the demise of the popular Germanicus tarnished Tiberius's reputation.

v.t: Widespread Uprisings in Hebei and Shandong
v.s: A wave of large-scale peasant uprisings broke out in Hebei and Shandong. The middle and lower reaches of the Yellow River suffered from floods, droughts, and oppressive government. Rebel groups ranged from tens of thousands to a few thousand, rising one after another.

v.t: Anti-Wang Mang Uprisings Swept the Country
v.s: Anti-Wang Mang uprisings spread from localized areas across the nation, from Hebei in the north to Jingzhou in the south, from Shandong in the east to Guanzhong in the west. Rebel groups rose everywhere, and the Xin dynasty's ruling system was on the verge of collapse.

v.t: Wang Mang Issued Strict Order: 'Those Who Dared Counterfeit Coins Would Be Executed'
v.s: Counterfeiting of coins became increasingly rampant among the people. Wang Mang issued a strict order: those who dared to counterfeit coins would be executed. Despite the harsh penalty, counterfeiting continued unabated, only exacerbating social conflicts.

v.t: Roman Emperor Tiberius Retired to Capri
v.s: In AD 21, Roman Emperor Tiberius left Rome and went into seclusion on the island of Capri in the Bay of Naples. He built twelve luxurious villas (named after Greek gods) on the island, controlling the empire's administration through letters and coded messages. Tiberius's seclusion allowed Sejanus, the Praetorian Prefect, to gain increasing power in Rome.

v.t: Sejanus Rose to Power in Rome
v.s: In AD 21, Sejanus, the Praetorian Prefect, became the de facto ruler of Rome while Tiberius was in seclusion on Capri. By controlling the Praetorian Guard and a network of informants, he eliminated political opponents, gradually purging supporters of Tiberius's son Drusus and Germanicus. Sejanus's power peaked in AD 23, but he was eventually executed on Tiberius's orders in AD 31.

v.t: Liu Xiu and Liu Yan Raised Troops at Chongling
v.s: A ninth-generation descendant of Emperor Gaozu of Han, Liu Xiu, together with his elder brother Liu Yan, gathered over 8,000 Han clan members and followers at Chongling to rise in rebellion against Wang Mang. This force later became the backbone for overthrowing the Xin dynasty and restoring the Han dynasty.

v.t: Lülin Army Decisively Defeated Xin Forces at the Bishui River
v.s: In the third year of Wang Mang's Dihuang era (AD 22), the Lülin Army defeated the Xin Mang government forces at the Bishui River (modern Biyang, Henan). The Lülin Army originated from starving peasants in Jingzhou, splitting into Xiàjiang (Lower River) and Xinshi (New Market) groups. The two groups defeated the Xin commanders Yan You and Chen Mao's forces at the Bishui, killing several thousand soldiers. This battle marked the Lülin Army's transition from guerrilla warfare to open confrontation with government forces. Subsequently, the Lülin Army supported Liu Xuan as Emperor Gengshi, becoming a major force in overthrowing the Xin dynasty.

v.t: Great Famine in Guanzhong, Cannibalism Reported
v.s: A severe famine struck the Guanzhong region, capital of the Xin dynasty. Grain prices skyrocketed, corpses littered the roads, and there were even reports of cannibalism. The Wang Mang government was unable to provide relief, and masses of starving people flooded into Chang'an seeking survival.

v.t: Sejanus's Power Reached Its Zenith
v.s: Sejanus, the Roman Praetorian Prefect, reached the peak of his power, controlling the Praetorian Guard, being granted consular authority, planting informants throughout Rome to eliminate political opponents, and intimidating the Senate into silence.

v.t: Battle of Kunyang - Liu Xiu's Rise to Fame
v.s: Wang Mang sent Grand Minister of Works Wang Yi and Minister over the Masses Wang Xun leading a force that was said to be a million strong to attack Kunyang. Liu Xiu led thirteen horsemen to break out and seek reinforcements, then personally led 3,000 daredevil troops to launch a surprise attack on the Mang army camp, achieving a great victory, one of the most famous instances in Chinese history of a smaller force defeating a larger one.

v.t: Lülin Army Supported Liu Xuan as Emperor (Emperor Gengshi)
v.s: The Lülin Army supported Liu Xuan as emperor at Wancheng, changing the era name to Gengshi. Liu Xuan was a cousin of Liu Xiu. After proclaiming himself emperor, Gengshi appointed Liu Xiu as General of the Left Flank. The establishment of the Gengshi regime provided a unified banner for the anti-Wang Mang forces.

v.t: Wang Mang Was Beheaded, Xin Dynasty Fell
v.s: In October, the Lülin Army entered Chang'an. Wang Mang fled in the chaos to the Terrace at Dutai (Jiantai), where he was killed by a merchant named Du Wu. His body was dismembered, and his head was sent around the country for public display. The Xin dynasty had lasted only fifteen years.

v.t: Liu Yan Killed by Emperor Gengshi
v.s: Emperor Gengshi, jealous of Liu Yan's prestige and ability within the Lülin Army, had him executed on charges of treason. Liu Yan was brave and strong-willed. After his brother was killed, Liu Xiu remained patient, not revealing his intentions, and apologized to Emperor Gengshi, saving his own life.

v.t: Tiberius and Sejanus Co-ruled
v.s: In AD 23, Tiberius granted Sejanus the Praetorian Prefect equal consular power ('imperium proconsulare'), making them formally co-rulers. Sejanus became the second most powerful person in the empire, second only to Tiberius. However, his ambitions aroused Tiberius's suspicions, ultimately leading to his downfall.

v.t: Liu Xiu Was Sent to Pacify Hebei
v.s: Emperor Gengshi sent Liu Xiu, with the title of General Who Breaks the Barbarians, to inspect and pacify the commanderies of Hebei. In Hebei, Liu Xiu abolished harsh policies, released prisoners, and recruited talented individuals. He gained the support of local powerful families, gradually building his own power base.

v.t: Liu Xiu Defeated Wang Lang and Captured Handan
v.s: In AD 24, Liu Xiu led his forces to defeat Wang Lang, a fortune teller who claimed to be a son of Emperor Cheng of Han and had proclaimed himself emperor in Handan. Wang Lang controlled most of Hebei. Supported by Emperor Gengshi, Liu Xiu attacked and captured Handan, killing Wang Lang. After this battle, Liu Xiu gained a firm foothold in Hebei and secured cavalry support from Shanggu and Yuyang commanderies, significantly increasing his military power.

v.t: Emperor Gengshi Moved the Capital to Chang'an
v.s: Emperor Gengshi moved the capital from Luoyang to Chang'an. As the former capital of the Western Han, Chang'an held political symbolic significance. However, after entering Chang'an, the Gengshi army's discipline broke down, and they looted the city, rapidly losing popular support.

v.t: Sejanus Persecuted Opposition
v.s: In AD 24, Sejanus systematically eliminated political opponents in Rome. Taking advantage of Tiberius's trust, he prosecuted numerous noble senators under the charge of 'maiestas' (treason). Many senators were executed or exiled, and their property confiscated. Sejanus also attempted to marry Tiberius's niece Livilla to become a member of the imperial family.

v.t: Liu Xiu Established the Eastern Han (Guangwu Restoration)
v.s: In June, Liu Xiu ascended the throne as emperor at the Qianqiu Pavilion in Hao City, changing the era name to Jianwu. The national title remained 'Han', and he established the capital at Luoyang (called Eastern Han because it was east of Chang'an). Liu Xiu was known as Emperor Guangwu of Han, and he initiated the 'Guangwu Restoration'.

v.t: Red Eyebrow Army Captured Chang'an, Gengshi Regime Fell
v.s: In AD 25, the Red Eyebrow Army entered Chang'an. Emperor Gengshi, Liu Xuan, surrendered and was subsequently killed, marking the end of the Gengshi regime. Led by Fan Chong, the Red Eyebrow Army supported Liu Penzi as emperor, but they looted and plundered in Chang'an, quickly losing popular support. In the same year, Liu Xiu declared himself emperor, establishing the Eastern Han dynasty.

v.t: Deng Yu Led His Army West into Guanzhong
v.s: In AD 25, Emperor Guangwu of Han sent Deng Yu with about 20,000 troops west into Guanzhong to attack the Red Eyebrow Army. Deng Yu defeated remnants of the Gengshi regime in Hedong and crossed the Yellow River into Guanzhong. However, his campaign against the Red Eyebrow Army progressed slowly, and he was defeated several times. Guangwu later replaced Deng Yu with Feng Yi as commander.

v.t: Caligula Lived with Tiberius on Capri
v.s: The young Caligula lived with Emperor Tiberius on the island of Capri, learning to disguise his emotions and feign obedience. This experience shaped his complex and volatile personality later in life.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Enfeoffed Founding Contributors
v.s: Emperor Guangwu of Han enfeoffed his founding contributors as marquises. The most meritorious, including Deng Yu (Marquis of Liang), Wu Han (Marquis of Guangping), and Jia Fu (Marquis of Jiaodong), totaling over three hundred individuals. He provided generous treatment to these contributors but granted them no real power, preventing the rise of powerful ministers.

v.t: Red Eyebrow Army Pillaged Chang'an and Moved West
v.s: With grain supplies exhausted in Guanzhong, the Red Eyebrow Army could not maintain their position and decided to withdraw westward. Before leaving, they pillaged Chang'an extensively, burning down the palaces and treasures accumulated by the Western Han over two centuries.

v.t: Peng Chong of Yuyang Rebelled
v.s: Peng Chong, Grand Administrator of Yuyang Commandery and a subordinate of Liu Xiu, rebelled due to dissatisfaction with Liu Xiu's suspicions. Peng Chong had been an important supporter of Liu Xiu in Hebei. His rebellion put Liu Xiu in a difficult position, having to fight on two fronts.

v.t: Sejanus Fell and Was Killed
v.s: Sejanus, the Roman Praetorian Prefect, was suddenly arrested and executed on Tiberius's orders. Tiberius's order was delivered by letter. Sejanus was read his list of crimes and executed on the spot in the Senate, and his followers were also purged.

v.t: Red Eyebrow Army Surrendered to Eastern Han
v.s: Over one hundred thousand remaining Red Eyebrow troops led by Fan Chong surrendered to Liu Xiu at Yiyang. Having been cornered by pursuing Eastern Han forces in the Longdong and Guanzhong regions, suffering from hunger and cold, the Red Eyebrow Army had nowhere to go.

v.t: Liu Xiu Eliminated Liu Yong's Forces
v.s: In the third year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 27), Emperor Guangwu Liu Xiu sent Generals Wu Han and Gai Yan to lead an eastern expedition against Liu Yong, who had seized Suiyang (modern Shangqiu, Henan). Liu Yong was a descendant of Liu Li, the King of Liang of the Western Han, and had declared himself Son of Heaven, occupying parts of eastern Henan and northern Jiangsu. Wu Han captured Suiyang, and Liu Yong fled to Qiao County (modern Bozhou, Anhui) where he was killed by his own subordinates. The destruction of Liu Yong's forces gave Liu Xiu control over most of Guandong, taking a major step towards unifying the Eastern Han dynasty.

v.t: Tiberius's Later Reign of Terror
v.s: In AD 27, the later reign of Roman Emperor Tiberius became increasingly tyrannical. From his seclusion on Capri, he had a great number of senators and nobles whom he suspected of conspiracy executed, using trials for 'maiestas' (treason) to arbitrarily punish political opponents. Informers thrived, and many were executed on mere suspicion. Tiberius's tyranny plunged Rome into a reign of terror.

v.t: Jesus Began His Ministry
v.s: Jesus of Nazareth began his public ministry in the region of Galilee, preaching the gospel of the kingdom of God, teaching repentance and love for others, and performing healings and miracles. His ministry attracted many followers but also aroused the suspicion of Jewish religious leaders.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Implemented Streamlining and Cost-Cutting
v.s: Liu Xiu issued an edict to consolidate commanderies and counties, reducing the number of officials. Over 400 counties were merged, a large number of local officials were dismissed, and central government institutions were streamlined, reducing government spending to one-tenth of that in the late Western Han.

v.t: Wu Han Pacified the Eastern Warlords
v.s: In AD 28, Liu Xiu's general Wu Han led forces to pacify various separatist regimes in the east. He successively defeated warlords Liu Yong, Dong Xian, and Pang Meng, who held sway in Shandong and Jiangsu. Wu Han was known for his bravery and ferocity in battle but was also criticized for his cruelty, sometimes massacring entire cities after capturing them. His victories allowed Liu Xiu to bring most of Guandong under his control.

v.t: Cen Peng's Southern Campaign Against Qin Feng
v.s: In AD 28, Liu Xiu sent Cen Peng to lead a southern expedition against Qin Feng, who had seized control of Nan Commandery (modern Hubei). Qin Feng fortified himself at Liqiu (modern Yicheng, Hubei). Cen Peng advanced south along the Han River using his naval superiority but encountered fierce resistance when besieging Liqiu. Qin Feng held out for over two years, only being captured in AD 30.

v.t: Tiberius Continued the Reign of Terror
v.s: In AD 28, Roman Emperor Tiberius continued to remotely control Rome's reign of terror from Capri. Numerous treason trials continued, and many senators were executed or forced to commit suicide. Informers were rewarded, and all of Roman society was enveloped in suspicion and fear. The power of Praetorian Prefect Sejanus continued to expand.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Reduced Land Tax to One-Thirtieth
v.s: In the fifth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 29), Emperor Guangwu issued an edict reducing the land tax from one-tenth of the harvest (the rate in the late Western Han) to one-thirtieth, matching the low tax rate of Emperor Wen of Han's reign. This policy greatly alleviated the burden on peasants and promoted the post-war recovery of agricultural production. The one-thirtieth tax rate became the standard land tax for the Eastern Han dynasty, remaining in effect until the end of the dynasty.

v.t: Re-established the Imperial Academy and Revived Confucianism
v.s: Liu Xiu ordered the reconstruction of the Imperial Academy in Luoyang, restoring the Confucian classics that had been damaged during the wars. The rebuilt academy gathered scholars from across the nation, the Erudites of the Five Classics resumed their teachings, and Confucianism was re-established as the official state ideology.

v.t: Peng Chong of Yuyang Killed by a Servant
v.s: In AD 29, Peng Chong, the warlord controlling Yuyang (modern Miyun, Beijing), was killed by one of his own household servants. Peng Chong had originally been a subordinate of Liu Xiu, but rebelled due to dissatisfaction with his rewards. His servant, Zimi, killed him while he slept and sent his head to Luoyang. The elimination of Peng Chong's forces removed a threat to Liu Xiu's northern rear.

v.t: John the Baptist Preached
v.s: John the Baptist preached in the region of the Jordan River, calling on people to repent and be baptized in preparation for the coming Messiah. John wore clothing made of camel's hair and ate locusts and wild honey. He was later imprisoned and beheaded for rebuking Herod Antipas for marrying his brother's wife.

v.t: The Crucifixion of Jesus
v.s: Pontius Pilate, the Roman governor of Judea, condemned Jesus of Nazareth to death by crucifixion in Jerusalem. Jesus was charged with inciting rebellion and calling himself King of the Jews. He was crucified at Golgotha, a hill outside the city. Christian faith holds that Jesus rose from the dead three days after his crucifixion and ascended to heaven. His death and resurrection are central doctrines of Christianity.

v.t: Implementation of the Land Survey Decree Began
v.s: Liu Xiu issued an edict implementing the Land Survey Decree (Dutian Ling) nationwide, ordering each commandery and county to survey and verify land holdings and household numbers. The aim was to fairly distribute the tax burden and increase government revenue. However, it infringed on the interests of local powerful families, leading to strong resistance.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Reduced Local Armed Forces
v.s: Liu Xiu ordered the reduction of local commandery and kingdom armed forces, abolishing the system of Commandery Commandants (Duwei) and having the Grand Administrators concurrently take charge of military affairs. This measure strengthened centralization but also left the Eastern Han lacking effective capabilities to deal with rebellions in its later period.

v.t: Expansion of the Kushan Empire
v.s: Around AD 30, the Kushan Empire, under the leadership of Kujula Kadphises, continued its expansion. Kushan forces occupied the Kabul River Valley and parts of the Indus River Valley, gaining control over the vital Silk Road transit hub. The Kushan Empire amassed vast wealth through trade with Rome and China, and the weight standard of its gold coins matched that of Roman gold coins.

v.t: Tiberius Summoned Caligula
v.s: Emperor Tiberius, living in seclusion on Capri, summoned the young Caligula to his side to be personally trained. On the island, Caligula learned to hide his true feelings and feign obedience.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Executed Officials for Falsifying Land Surveys
v.s: Discovering widespread falsification in the land surveys (Dutian), Liu Xiu severely punished the responsible officials. The Grand Minister of Education, Ouyang Xi, was imprisoned and executed, and many other officials were also put to death. Liu Xiu used harsh measures to enforce the implementation of the land survey decree.

v.t: Du Shi Invented the Water-Powered Blower
v.s: Du Shi, Grand Administrator of Nanyang during the Eastern Han, summarized the experiences of the working people and invented the water-powered bellows (Shui Pai). This device used a water wheel to drive leather bellows, providing a blast of air for iron smelting furnaces, greatly improving efficiency, and predating similar European technology by about 1100 years.

v.t: Christianity Began to Spread to Gentiles
v.s: After the descent of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost, the disciples of Jesus began to expand the spread of the gospel from the Jewish community to Gentiles. The Apostle Peter baptized Cornelius, a Roman centurion, marking Christianity's breakthrough of Jewish ethno-religious boundaries.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Personally Led Campaign Against Wei Xiao
v.s: Liu Xiu personally led a major expedition against the separatist Wei Xiao in Longxi. Wei Xiao outwardly submitted to the Eastern Han but secretly colluded with Gongsun Shu. Liu Xiu decided to take advantage of Wei Xiao's unstable position to pacify Longxi once and for all.

v.t: Wei Xiao Sought Help from Gongsun Shu
v.s: In AD 32, Wei Xiao, the warlord controlling Longxi, sought help from Gongsun Shu, who had seized the Shu region, under military pressure from Liu Xiu. Gongsun Shu enfeoffed Wei Xiao as King of Shuoning and sent troops to reinforce him. Liu Xiu personally led his army against Longxi, defeating the combined forces of Wei Xiao and Gongsun Shu at Lüeyang. Wei Xiao retreated to Luomen (modern Wushan, Tianshui) and died of illness in AD 33.

v.t: Jesus Predicted the Destruction of Jerusalem
v.s: According to the Bible, Jesus predicted on the Mount of Olives that the Temple in Jerusalem would be completely destroyed. This prophecy was fulfilled in AD 70 when Roman legions captured Jerusalem.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Reduced Land Tax to One-Thirtieth [33]
v.s: In the ninth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 33), Emperor Guangwu once again issued an edict reaffirming the land tax of one-thirtieth of the harvest, ordering all commanderies and kingdoms to strictly implement it. This policy was an important part of Emperor Guangwu's 'rest and recuperation' approach, providing institutional guarantee for the economic recovery of the early Eastern Han.

v.t: Wu Han and Others Attacked Longxi
v.s: Grand Marshal Wu Han led the main Eastern Han army in a fierce attack on Longxi, successively capturing the cities held by Wei Chun. The victory in the Longxi campaign brought the Hexi Corridor under Eastern Han control, opening the route to the Western Regions.

v.t: Wei Xiao Died of Illness, Wei Chun Succeeded
v.s: In the ninth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 33), Wei Xiao, the warlord controlling Longxi (eastern Gansu), died of illness. Wei Xiao had originally been a subordinate of Emperor Gengshi, wavering between Liu Xiu and Gongsun Shu. Before his death, Wei Xiao passed his power to his son Wei Chun, who continued to resist Eastern Han unification. Emperor Guangwu took advantage of Wei Xiao's recent death and internal instability to launch a massive offensive between AD 33-34, ultimately capturing Luomenju (modern Wushan, Tianshui) where Wei Chun was holding out. Wei Chun surrendered, and Longxi was pacified.

v.t: Church Development After Jesus's Crucifixion and Resurrection
v.s: According to Christian tradition, Jesus was crucified and resurrected around AD 30. Around AD 33, Jesus's disciples Peter and Paul began preaching in Jerusalem and Antioch, and Christianity gradually evolved from a small Jewish sect into a new religion. Paul undertook three missionary journeys in the AD 40-50s, spreading Christianity to Asia Minor, Greece, and Rome.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Issued Edicts to Free Slaves
v.s: Emperor Guangwu issued multiple edicts to free slaves, stipulating that those who had been sold into slavery due to the wars could regain their civilian status. These measures helped alleviate social conflicts and restore productivity.

v.t: Han Forces Captured Luomen, Wei Chun Surrendered
v.s: In the tenth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 34), Emperor Guangwu sent generals Lai Xi and Geng Yan to lead forces in besieging Wei Chun's last stronghold at Luomen (modern Wushan County, Tianshui). The Han forces used siege engines in continuous, fierce attacks. Wei Chun's subordinates, including Wang Yuan, surrendered. Seeing that the situation was hopeless, Wei Chun was forced to surrender. Longxi was thus completely pacified. By this time, Liu Xiu had eliminated all the separatist regimes except for Gongsun Shu, and the unification of the Eastern Han was essentially complete.

v.t: Jewish Governor Pilate Suppressed the Samaritans
v.s: In AD 34, the Roman governor of Judea, Pontius Pilate, led forces to suppress a religious gathering of Samaritans on Mount Gerizim, where they claimed to have discovered sacred vessels of Moses. Pilate attacked, using the pretext that they were assembling to rebel, killing many. This led to Pilate being recalled to Rome for trial in AD 36.

v.t: Cen Peng Defeated Gongsun Shu's Forces
v.s: In the eleventh year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 35), Eastern Han general Cen Peng led a naval force up the Yangtze River to attack Gongsun Shu, who had seized Yizhou (modern Sichuan). Cen Peng decisively defeated Gongsun Shu's naval forces at Jingmen (modern Yichang, Hubei), capturing many warships. He then led his forces upstream, capturing Ba Commandery (modern Chongqing) and advancing on Chengdu. Cen Peng's successive victories paved the way for Liu Xiu to ultimately pacify Gongsun Shu and unify the empire. However, Cen Peng was assassinated the following year (AD 36) by an assassin sent by Gongsun Shu.

v.t: Lai Xi Assassinated
v.s: In the eleventh year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 35), Lai Xi, a general of the Eastern Han, was assassinated by an assassin sent by Gongsun Shu while campaigning against him. Lai Xi was an important general of Liu Xiu, tasked with attacking Gongsun Shu together with Cen Peng. After capturing Hechi (modern Huixian, Gansu), Lai Xi was marching south when he was severely wounded in his camp by one of Gongsun Shu's assassins. On his deathbed, Lai Xi summoned Gai Yan and others, entrusted them with military affairs, then drew his sword and ended his own life in a heroic manner. Liu Xiu wept bitterly upon hearing the news.

v.t: Roman Emperor Tiberius Died, Caligula Succeeded
v.s: In AD 37, Roman Emperor Tiberius died of illness on Capri at the age of 78. Caligula, only 25 years old, succeeded him. In his early reign, Caligula enacted benevolent policies (declaring an amnesty, reducing taxes), winning the love of the people and the Senate. However, after a serious illness, his character changed drastically, and he began a reign of terror.

v.t: Wu Han Captured Chengdu, Gongsun Shu Perished
v.s: In AD 36, Grand Marshal Wu Han led his forces to capture Chengdu. Gongsun Shu was killed in battle, and the Shu region was pacified. Liu Xiu had eliminated the last separatist force in the unification wars, achieving basic unification of the country. The territory of the Eastern Han was established, and Liu Xiu began to implement a policy of 'laying down arms and cultivating culture'.

v.t: Liu Xiu Implemented the Policy of 'Laying Down Arms and Cultivating Culture'
v.s: After achieving basic unification of the country in AD 36, Emperor Guangwu implemented a policy of 'laying down arms and cultivating culture' (Yanwu Xiuwen). A large number of soldiers were demobilized and sent back to their villages to farm, local armed forces were reduced, while Confucianism was promoted and the Imperial Academy was expanded. Emperor Guangwu proposed 'retiring the meritorious and advancing the civil officials', marginalizing the military merit group from core decision-making and relying heavily on civilian officials and Confucian scholars.

v.t: Eastern Han Achieved Basic Unification
v.s: After twelve years of warfare, Emperor Guangwu of Eastern Han, after eliminating the last separatist regime of Gongsun Shu in AD 36, basically completed the unification of the empire. Liu Xiu had declared himself emperor and established the Eastern Han at Hao County (modern Gaoyi, Hebei) in AD 25, then successively eliminated the Red Eyebrow Army, Liu Yong, Peng Chong, Zhang Bu, Wei Xiao, Gongsun Shu, and other separatist forces. Liu Xiu adopted the policy of 'retiring the meritorious and advancing the civil officials', strengthening local control and restoring centralized political order, known as the 'Guangwu Restoration'.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu's Lenient Policy of Not Killing Meritorious Officials
v.s: Emperor Guangwu of Eastern Han was known for 'not killing meritorious officials', in stark contrast to Emperor Gaozu of Han. Almost all of Liu Xiu's twenty-eight founding meritorious officials (the Twenty-Eight Generals of Yuntai) died natural deaths. He adopted a strategy of enfeoffing meritorious officials as marquises with no real power, recognizing their contributions while preventing them from interfering in court politics. This policy stabilized the political situation in the early Eastern Han.

v.t: Kujula Kadphises of the Kushan Formally Proclaimed King
v.s: Around AD 37, Kujula Kadphises, chief of the Kushan tribe (one of the five Yuezhi Xihou), united the other four tribes, established the Kushan Empire in the Bactria region, and formally proclaimed himself 'King of Kings', establishing his capital at Bactra (modern Balkh, Afghanistan). He issued coins in the Roman style and amassed vast wealth through Silk Road trade. At its peak, the Kushan Empire encompassed parts of modern Afghanistan, Pakistan, and northern India.

v.t: Caligula's Personality Changed After Serious Illness
v.s: In AD 37, Roman Emperor Caligula suffered a serious illness, and after his recovery, his character completely changed. The young, diligent, and popular emperor turned into a tyrannical dictator. He executed opponents, proclaimed himself a god, and forced his subjects to worship him. Economically, Caligula was wildly extravagant, exhausting the vast treasury reserves left by Tiberius in less than four years.

v.t: Full Recovery of Social Production in Eastern Han
v.s: In the fourteenth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 38), after more than a decade of rest, recuperation, and production restoration under Emperor Guangwu, the Eastern Han social economy fully recovered. Emperor Guangwu promoted land surveys (Dutian), reduced land taxes to one-thirtieth, freed slaves, built water conservancy projects, and promoted the use of iron plows and oxen for farming. The population gradually rebounded from the slump of the late Western Han, and the output of agriculture and handicrafts recovered to the levels of the Western Han peak.

v.t: Caligula's Relationship with the Senate Deteriorated
v.s: In AD 38, Caligula's relationship with the Roman Senate deteriorated sharply. He abolished many of the Senate's traditional privileges and forced senators to prostrate themselves before him. Caligula publicly humiliated members of the Senate and murdered many senators. He even appointed his horse 'Incitatus' as a consul to demonstrate his contempt for the Senate.

v.t: The Arsacid Empire Expanded Eastward
v.s: In AD 38, the Arsacid (Parthian) Empire, under the rule of Artabanus III, expanded eastward, consolidating control over the Khorasan and Margiana regions. The spheres of influence of the Arsacid and Kushan empires came into contact in Central Asia. The two states maintained trade relations and some diplomatic contact.

v.t: Land Survey Decree Led to Serious Political Crisis
v.s: In the fifteenth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 39), Emperor Guangwu ordered a nationwide land survey (Dutian). Local officials colluded with powerful families to conceal large amounts of land and shift the tax burden onto the peasants. The peasants, unable to bear the heavy burden, rose in unrest. Emperor Guangwu sent investigators, and after discovering the fraud, executed over ten corrupt officials, including the Administrator of Henan, Zhang Ji, quelling the crisis.

v.t: Caligula Declared Himself a God
v.s: In AD 39, Caligula openly declared himself a god, demanding that the Roman people and the provinces worship him. He set up statues of himself in Rome and Jerusalem, which provoked strong resistance from the Jews. Caligula even ordered a statue of himself to be placed in the Temple in Jerusalem, but this order was not carried out due to his death (AD 41).

v.t: Outbreak of the Trưng Sisters' Rebellion in Jiaozhi
v.s: In the sixteenth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 40), the Trưng Sisters (Trưng Trắc and Trưng Nhị) in Jiaozhi Commandery (northern Vietnam) launched a rebellion due to the oppressive rule of the Eastern Han official Su Ding. The Trưng Sisters led the local people to capture the commandery seat, and the commanderies of Jiuzhen and Rinan responded one after another. The rebel forces took control of a large area of northern Vietnam. Trưng Trắc declared herself queen and continued to resist Eastern Han rule.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Acknowledged Reality of Land Survey and Quelled Unrest
v.s: In the sixteenth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 40), disturbances arose across the country due to the falsification of land surveys (Dutian) by local officials and the oppression of the people. Emperor Guangwu sent officials to inspect, acknowledged the shortcomings in implementing the Land Survey Decree, executed over ten corrupt officials including the Administrator of Henan, Zhang Ji, and adjusted policies. This quelled the political crisis caused by the land survey and restored social order.

v.t: Caligula's Farce on the Rhine Frontier
v.s: In AD 40, Caligula led a large army to the Rhine frontier, claiming to launch an expedition against the Germanic tribes. However, he merely had his troops 'fight a naval battle' on the coast (collecting seashells as 'spoils'), then returned to Rome. This ridiculous expedition exposed Caligula's mental instability and wasted a huge amount of military funds.

v.t: Caligula Assassinated, Claudius Became Emperor
v.s: Roman Emperor Caligula was assassinated by the Praetorian Guard. The Praetorians discovered Claudius (Caligula's uncle) hiding in the palace and proclaimed him emperor. Although physically disabled, Claudius became a capable ruler, completing the conquest of Britain, expanding the port of Ostia, and achieving other successes.

v.t: Emperor Guangwu Established Guo Shengtong as Empress
v.s: During the establishment of the Eastern Han dynasty, to gain the support of powerful families in Hebei, Emperor Guangwu of Han married Guo Shengtong, the niece of Liu Yang, the Prince of Zhending, in the second year of the Jianwu era (AD 26), and later established her as Empress. Guo Shengtong came from a prestigious family in Hebei and played an important role in solidifying Liu Xiu's Hebei base. However, Liu Xiu favored his original wife Yin Lihua more, and believing that Guo Shengtong 'had no son' (though she did have a son, Liu Qiang), he deposed Guo Shengtong in the seventeenth year of the Jianwu era (AD 41) and established Yin Lihua as Empress instead. Guo Shengtong thus became the first deposed empress in Chinese history.

v.t: Ma Yuan Led an Expedition Against Jiaozhi
v.s: In the eighteenth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 42), Emperor Guangwu sent General of the Waves Who Subdues the South (Fubo Jiangjun) Ma Yuan to lead a combined naval and land force of about 20,000 men to suppress the Trưng Sisters' rebellion in Jiaozhi. Ma Yuan marched south along the coast and engaged the main force of the Trưng Sisters at Lang Bac (near modern Hanoi, Vietnam). Ma Yuan defeated the rebel forces with his superior numbers, killing several thousand.

v.t: Claudius Built a New Port at Ostia
v.s: Roman Emperor Claudius built an artificial port at Ostia, at the mouth of the Tiber River. The port featured huge breakwaters and a lighthouse, capable of accommodating large grain ships, greatly reducing grain transport costs. It was a masterpiece of Roman public works.

v.t: Claudius Conquered Britain
v.s: Roman Emperor Claudius sent his general Aulus Plautius with a force of 40,000 men to invade Britain. They landed in Kent and defeated the local resistance. Claudius personally went to Britain to accept the surrender. By AD 47, the Romans controlled southern and central England and established the province of Britannia, which would last for nearly four hundred years.

v.t: Ma Yuan Defeated the Trưng Sisters, Beheading Them
v.s: In AD 43, General Ma Yuan pursued the retreating forces of the Trưng Sisters in Jiaozhi. He defeated their last resistance at Cấm Khê (modern Thanh Hóa, Vietnam), where the Trưng Sisters were captured and executed. Ma Yuan sent the heads of the Trưng Sisters to Luoyang as proof of victory, and the Jiaozhi rebellion was basically pacified.

v.t: Ma Yuan Returned to the Court from Jiaozhi
v.s: In the twentieth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 44), Ma Yuan set up a bronze pillar in Jiaozhi to mark the southernmost boundary of the Han dynasty before leading his troops back to the court. During his time in Jiaozhi, he reorganized local administration and promoted Han agricultural and water conservancy techniques. Emperor Guangwu awarded Ma Yuan substantial gifts upon his return and enfeoffed him as Marquis of Xinxi. However, in his later years, Ma Yuan was drawn into political struggles and was misunderstood by Emperor Guangwu.

v.t: King Herod Agrippa I of Judea Died
v.s: In AD 44, King Herod Agrippa I of Judea suddenly died of abdominal pain during a public event in Caesarea. He had assisted Emperor Claudius in ascending the throne in AD 41 and had been granted rule over all of Judea. After Agrippa I's death, the region of Judea was once again placed under the direct rule of a Roman governor.

v.t: Wuhuan and Xianbei Raided the Frontier
v.s: In the twenty-first year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 45), the northern Wuhuan and Xianbei tribes jointly raided the Eastern Han border, plundering Yuyang, Shanggu, and other commanderies. Emperor Guangwu sent General Ma Yuan with 3,000 troops to ride out and defend the frontier. Ma Yuan, pursuing with infantry, encountered a surprise attack by the enemy cavalry and suffered heavy losses. The Eastern Han then adopted a strategy of 'using barbarians to control barbarians', supporting the Xianbei to strike at the Wuhuan. However, the border disturbances were never completely resolved.

v.t: The Apostle Paul Began His First Missionary Journey
v.s: The Apostle Paul set out from Antioch on his first missionary journey, traveling to Cyprus and Asia Minor, establishing several churches, and spreading Christianity to the Gentile world.

v.t: Xiongnu Split, Southern Xiongnu Submitted to Eastern Han
v.s: In AD 48, after the death of the Xiongnu Chanyu Yu, the Xiongnu split into northern and southern factions due to internal strife and natural disasters. The Southern Xiongnu Chanyu Huhanye led over 40,000 people to submit to the Eastern Han. Emperor Guangwu settled them in the Hetao region (modern Inner Mongolia and northern Shanxi), making them a tributary of the Eastern Han.

v.t: King Xian of Shache Dominated the Western Regions
v.s: In AD 46, King Xian of the Shache Kingdom (modern Shache, Xinjiang) achieved dominance over the Western Regions through military expansion. He successively conquered states such as Khotan and Kashgar, establishing a hegemonic position on the southern route of the Western Regions. King Xian sent envoys to the Eastern Han to declare submission while maintaining de facto independence. Xian's expansion threatened Eastern Han control over the Western Regions.

v.t: Southern Xiongnu Formally Submitted to Eastern Han
v.s: In the twenty-third year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 47), Southern Xiongnu Chanyu Huhanye formally sent his son to Luoyang as a hostage, marking the Southern Xiongnu's status as a tributary of the Eastern Han. Emperor Guangwu established the Chanyu's court at Wuyuan Fortress (modern Baotou, Inner Mongolia), later moving it to Meiji County in Xihe Commandery. The Southern Xiongnu received annual grain and material aid from the Eastern Han.

v.t: Wuling's Five Streams Barbarians Rebelled
v.s: In the twenty-third year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 47), the Five Streams Barbarians (Wuxi Man) tribe in Wuling Commandery (western Hunan) rebelled due to oppressive taxes and corvée labor imposed by local officials. Emperor Guangwu successively sent generals Liu Shang and Li Song to lead expeditions, but they were repeatedly defeated due to unfamiliarity with the mountainous terrain. Finally, the Vanguard General Ma Yuan was dispatched with 40,000 troops. Ma Yuan adopted a cautious strategy and established his camp at Hutou Mountain (modern Yuanling), but his campaign was hampered by an epidemic in the army. After Ma Yuan died of illness, the Eastern Han adjusted its policy, focusing on pacification, and did not completely quell the rebellion until AD 49.

v.t: Claudius Restored the Office of Censor
v.s: Roman Emperor Claudius restored the office of Censor, which had been dormant for decades since Augustus's reign, serving as Censor himself. He purged the Senate and brought Gallic nobles into it, expanding his base of support.

v.t: Roman Soldiers Built a Fortress at Utrecht
v.s: In AD 47, the Roman Empire built a series of fortresses along the Rhine frontier in the province of Germania. Roman soldiers established a fortress at Traiectum (modern Utrecht, Netherlands). This was part of the Roman Rhine defensive line. The surrounding Germanic tribes maintained trade relations with Rome.

v.t: Ma Yuan Volunteered to Campaign Against the Five Streams Barbarians
v.s: The 62-year-old veteran general Ma Yuan volunteered to lead the campaign against the Five Streams Barbarians, and Emperor Guangwu agreed. Ma Yuan led his army on the expedition, but due to tactical errors and unsuitable climate, the campaign bogged down.

v.t: Council of Jerusalem
v.s: The Apostles Paul and Barnabas went to Jerusalem and met with church leaders Peter and James, among others. The council decided that Gentile Christians need not observe all Jewish laws. This marked the beginning of Christianity's development as a separate religion from Judaism.

v.t: Ma Yuan Died of Illness at Wuling
v.s: In the twenty-fifth year of the Jianwu era of Eastern Han (AD 49), General Ma Yuan died of illness in the army while campaigning against the Five Streams Barbarians at Wuling, at the age of sixty-four. Ma Yuan was one of the founding contributors of the Eastern Han dynasty, having fought numerous battles throughout his life. The idiom 'wrapped in a horse's hide' (dying on the battlefield) originated from his words. After his death, slandered by Liang Song, Emperor Guangwu revoked his title of Marquis of Xinxi.

v.t: Claudius Expelled the Jews from Rome
v.s: Due to conflicts between Jews and Christians, Roman Emperor Claudius ordered the expulsion of all Jews from Rome. This event led Jewish Christians such as Aquila and Priscilla to move to Corinth, where they met Paul.

v.t: London (Londinium) Founded
v.s: The Roman Empire established a settlement called Londinium on the banks of the River Thames. Due to its convenient water transportation, it quickly became the largest city in the province of Britannia, later developing into the world metropolis of London.

v.t: Cologne Elevated to a Roman City
